examples.title: CYK Algorithm for Ambiguity Detection (Undecidable - Shows Why)
examples.description: The CYK algorithm can detect if a string has multiple parse trees by finding multiple distinct parse structures, but this doesn't solve general ambiguity since we can't test all infinitely many strings.
examples.explanation: For a given CFG in CNF and string w, CYK computes all possible parse trees. If more than one distinct parse tree exists for any derivation of w, the grammar is ambiguous for that string. However, we cannot conclude ambiguity of grammar from testing finitely many strings – ambiguity might only manifest for longer strings (unbounded). The algorithm correctly identifies multiple parses but cannot prove unambiguity (would require infinite testing).
examples.timeComplexity: O(n³ × |P|) where n = string length, |P| = number of productions
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n²) for CYK table storing parse possibilities

examples.title: Precedence-Based Expression Grammar Parser
examples.description: Unambiguous grammar parser skeleton showing precedence enforcement
examples.code:
BEGIN
  FUNCTION parse_expression()
    RETURN parse_add()
  END
  
  FUNCTION parse_add()
    result ← parse_mult()
    WHILE current_token IN {'+', '-'} DO
      op ← current_token
      current_token ← next_token()
      right ← parse_mult()
      result ← Node(op, result, right)
    ENDWHILE
    RETURN result
  END
  
  FUNCTION parse_mult()
    result ← parse_primary()
    WHILE current_token IN {'*', '/'} DO
      op ← current_token
      current_token ← next_token()
      right ← parse_primary()
      result ← Node(op, result, right)
    ENDWHILE
    RETURN result
  END
  
  FUNCTION parse_primary()
    IF current_token = '(' THEN
      current_token ← next_token()
      result ← parse_expression()
      expect(')')
    ELSE
      result ← Node('id', value)
    ENDIF
    RETURN result
  END
END
examples.explanation: This recursive descent parser implements the unambiguous grammar E→E+T|T, T→T*F|F, F→(E)|id. Each precedence level (expression, term, factor) is handled by separate functions. The left-associativity is enforced by processing operators in a left-recursive while loop, building left-deep trees. This guarantees exactly one parse tree for each valid expression.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for parsing an expression of length n
examples.spaceComplexity: O(h) where h = parse tree height (O(log n) for balanced expressions)

examples.title: Bottom-Up Parser Conflict Resolution
examples.description: LR parser skeleton showing how shift-reduce conflicts are resolved using precedence and associativity
examples.code:
BEGIN
  FUNCTION resolve_shift_reduce(state, token)
    IF conflict_type = 'shift-reduce' THEN
      IF operator_precedence(token) > top_of_stack_precedence() THEN
        RETURN SHIFT
      ELSEIF operator_precedence(token) < top_of_stack_precedence() THEN
        RETURN REDUCE
      ELSE
        IF associativity(token) = LEFT THEN
          RETURN REDUCE
        ELSE
          RETURN SHIFT
        ENDIF
      ENDIF
    ENDIF
    RETURN DEFAULT_ACTION
  END
  
  FUNCTION resolve_reduce_reduce(state, token)
    FOR each production IN conflict_reductions DO
      IF production.precedence > best_production.precedence THEN
        best_production ← production
      ENDIF
    ENDFOR
    RETURN REDUCE(best_production)
  END
END
examples.explanation: In bottom-up parsing (LR(1), LALR), ambiguity is resolved by conflict resolution rules: (1) Shift-reduce: precedence decides; (2) Reduce-reduce: higher precedence production wins; (3) For equal precedence, associativity resolves: left-associative prefers reduce, right-associative prefers shift. This mechanical disambiguation replaces the ambiguous grammar with a deterministic parsing strategy.
